speaker: interviewer
text: --date is January 19th, 1997. Liberator is Dr. Brendan Phibbs. Interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Tucson, in the State of Arizona. Country is United States of America, and the language is in English. My name is Louise Bobrow, B-O-B-R-O-W. Today's date is January 19th, 1997. I am conducting an interview with liberator Dr. Brendan Phibbs. The interview is being conducted in Tucson, Arizona in the United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brendan Pearse Phibbs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you kindly spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-R-E-N-D-A-N, first name. Pearse, P-E-A-R-S-E. Phibbs, P-H-I-B-B-S. The middle name is for Patrick Pearse, the Irish patriot who was killed by the English. Had to slip that one in.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just shortening of Brendon to Bren or something like that. Red, when I was a cowboy at the age of 16 up in Wyoming, when I had red hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 3rd, 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 80 last month.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yonkers, New York, outside New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harry Clandillon Phibbs. C-L-A-N-D-I-L-L-O-N, old Irish connection.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He owned an advertising agency in Chicago. He came here as a young artist from Ireland at the age of 20-some, landed with $25-- the equivalent of $25 in  his pocket. Didn't know a soul in the Western hemisphere, up in Newfoundland. And made it-- did many things. Ended up owning a medical advertising agency that did professional ads in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah. He was short, endlessly enthusiastic, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dedicated to arts, culture, the outdoors, imaginative, could be a stern father when he so chose in the old country tradition.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's name was Therese Kelly. She was from Galway, Lough Corrib, Galway, Ireland. And she died when I was only eight years old of breast cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? No, she was a housewife. Actually, she did. She helped my father start his business. She was his secretary and entire office staff when they opened the Harry C. Phibbs Advertising Agency with $200 in the bank, and made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tall, very pretty, dark, very vivacious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any special fond memories you'd like to tell about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, let me think. I can remember my mother singing a favorite song to me when I was a little boy and leading me through the forests of a place in Indiana, where we had a cabin on Lake Michigan. And I remember my father tactfully organizing wolf hunts near this cabin with-- we-- they weren't wolves, of course. But I was solemnly presented with a sharpened stick when I was six years old and told, go out wolf hunting. And I'd hear wolves howling. Sure enough, you'd hear wolves howling all around, and so on. They organized kind of a magic life for a little boy, in many ways.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one brother. My sister's dead. My older sister, four years, died of cancer some years ago. My young half-brother, Rod-- my father remarried many-- some years later. And he is a very famous pediatrician, one of the most famous in the world. He's the head of the newborn department at the University of California. He lives out near San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Roderick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- was your brother in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rod was never in the military. He kind of was just the wrong age for everything. He was a little boy in World War II. He's 15 years younger than I am, so he fell out between all the different wars.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to the usual grammar schools in Chicago and went to DePaul High School, taught by the Vincentian brothers, in-- near outside of Chicago. My father's family are all Church of Ireland Anglican, which is high Episcopalians. My mother's were Catholics. And I naturally took to my father's church, but out of respect to my mother I went to Catholic schools, so I went to the school-- a boys school taught by Vincentian brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you moved from Yonkers to Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: I grew up in Chicago. We've moved there when I was a little tiny child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you attended through high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then went to Northwestern University, premed and medical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that all before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- ho would you describe, then, your religious upbringing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Say that again. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you raised a Catholic or were you raised Episcopalian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was raised between two churches, actually. The church I liked was St. James Episcopal Cathedral, where a nice old lady used to give me Scotch bread. And-- but to keep my mother, sister, and the others happy, I would pro forma go to the Catholic school, so I'm kind of an expert on theology in a couple of lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much did you know about Jews and Judaism growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, lots, because we lived, first on the near-- the middle north side of Chicago, Albany, Ravenswood area. And there were many, many of the Jews who had left Russia in the pogroms had settled there. So I remember seeing the Hebrew letters on stores. This was on Montrose Avenue, and that part of Chicago. And many of my playmates were Jewish boys. And-- some of my father's closest friends were Jews who were in the medical publishing, [? Mal ?] [? and Jay ?] [? Herz ?] of Minneapolis, and others. And so I remember sitting around our huge dining room table with the silver and the crystal, and long discussions of the theater and books and letters. That's when I-- I was teasing some Jewish friends of mine not long ago, said you can't be Jewish and be conservative, sorry. Jewish means humanistic, cultured, progressive, forward-looking, liberal. [LAUGHS] That's what it was around our house anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the prevailing attitude amongst the non-Jews in your community towards Jews when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was some mindless anti-Semitism, I think, among little boys. They didn't know what they were saying. They'd yell, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], just as a war cry as they ran down the street, throwing rocks at each other. But they didn't know what the words meant, but they'd heard them. Beyond that, I don't think there was anything very vocal or deliberate or organized.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any organizations prior to the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the Democratic Party, to the great discomfort of my father, who thought that anyone who was a New Deal Roosevelt Democrat was right on the skirts of Moscow or something. But that was about the size of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outdoors, all my life, besides reading, avid reading. But we used to go on canoe trips in Ontario. We had a summer home in the Indiana sand dunes, which was very wild country then. And even as a little boy, I could take care of myself in the forest. I learned how to paddle a canoe, how to live in the woods. And the summer I got out of high school, through some connections I had a job as a $30 a month cowboy on a ranch near Cody, Wyoming, I mean a real cattle ranch, where I really worked my little teenage tail off. But that's-- the outdoors, literature, music. I love music all my life. Even though I don't play a piano very well, I collect music.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play any other instrument?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the piano. I tried the violin, and it was enough to make you forswear music forever. We finally gave it away to our worst enemy, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you always aspire as a youngster to be a doctor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, ever since I was a little boy. It seemed perfectly natural. That's what I was supposed to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you marry prior to entering the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was married my senior year in medical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Penny, my wife, was from a New York family, old New England, old-- actually, she was from a descendant of the first settlers at-- on Nantucket, the Coffins.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Coffin. Well, her name was Harle, but they were direct descendants of Robert-- of Tristram Coffin, the original 16-something man on Nantucket, Old American.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children before you went into the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we have-- well, after I went in the military, not before. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after you went into the military, what did your wife do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was an Army wife. She lived in Army camps with us, had two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you enter the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was shortly after Pearl Harbor. I interned in the year July 1, '41 to July 1, '42. That's how the internship years run, July to July. And we had all registered for the draft already, so we knew we'd be going in the service sometime. But December 7th came along. And in the horrible weeks after that, we were losing the war so badly all over the Pacific and everywhere in the world that I went down to Fifth Army Headquarters, about February, I guess it was, and said, look, I want to get sworn in. I want to do everything I can to facilitate this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, delighted. Northwestern University, St. Luke's Hospital, Chicago, which is where I was interning. And I was a first lieutenant, Army United States Medical Corps. I got my orders. They said, finish your internship, and then you'll get your orders. So I got my orders August 1st, 1942 to the Medical Field Service School at Carlisle Barracks, Pennsylvania. Is that where you received, then, your basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basic training.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any specialized training there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was specialized for medical officers. That's what it was. It was the headquarters of the Medical Corps of the US Army. So they showed-- besides teaching us marching and drill and push-ups and the usual nonsense, we learned how the medical support of fighting units was ordered. This was a school for doctors who would be with combat units, not with hospitals, or anything like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then where did you go after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I was assigned to the 12th Armored Division, Camp Campbell, Tennessee.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your rank at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: First lieutenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to Campbell--

speaker: interviewee
text: To CAMP Campbell. And I spent two years-- we spent two years in training. And it takes about two years to really train a division, especially an armored division with a lot of specialist skills involved.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you-- what were you specifically trained-- training there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were training-- as armored force medics, we had to learn vehicle maintenance. We had half-tracks, which is half armored and half wheels, as well as regular cross-country ambulances, Jeeps, weapons carrier. We had to learn weapons convoy discipline, care of the wounded under difficult situations, blackout, how to set up an aid station in the forest or in a cellar or something and black it out from enemy air and enemy patrols, and front line surgery. But we were very front line, so what we did was pretty fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your unit was called the--

speaker: interviewee
text: 12th Armored Division.

speaker: interviewer
text: 12th Armored Division. And who was your commanding officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: The final man who took us into battle was Roderick Allen, General Roderick Allen.

speaker: interviewer
text: The members of your unit, where were they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Northerners. They were from Pennsylvania, Illinois. We had some hillbillies from the Appalachian area and a few Texans, who always took a lot of ribbing, because we sent-- our second year training was at Camp Barkeley, Texas, west of Abilene, which is a pretty dismal place, as we never tired pointing out to the Texans in our division.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jews in your unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Many of them in the medical battalion, the medical-- because many of them had been looking at professional careers. We had many Jews in the battalion as medical officers and dental officers. And then scattered through the division was about-- a pretty good cross-section of American population, except for the fact that we were segregated. There were no blacks at that point. We had a segregated Army in World War II.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many doctors would you say were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me think. There were three infantry battalions, which had two surgeons each. That's six. There were three tanks with one man each, which is nine. Excuse me, I've been fighting the flu. Three artillery battalions would be 12. And there were about-- one in the recons, 13. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the medical battalion, there were 3 companies, 12-- 20-some physicians total, I would guess in the division. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Excuse me. I'm fighting the current version of the flu for a few days. I have to cough. I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: --episodes of anti-Semitism within your unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gosh. I don't think so, because we had so many Jewish officers in the unit, that is, in the medical battalion. In the unit generally, not anything overt that I can ever recall, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did everybody in the unit get along well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Everyone was accepted on the basis of how he performed as an officer. In the Army, you can't disguise things very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in combat, finally when I went overseas, my closest friend was a man named Clarence Sebralsky, who's now a lawyer in Green Bay, Wisconsin. And some of the pictures we're going to see today were taken by him. He was the signal officer of our combat command headquarters. And a man we nicknamed Panchko, Pencho Punski, who now lives in New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we called him Poncho for so long I forgot what Punski's first name was. But he was a Jewish boy from New York who we used to say looked and talked like Groucho Marx, even in combat. He was a very good man. Two of my closest friends, I guess, in the army. And then we had our little-- before I went overseas, I was promoted. First, I made captain, company commander. And then I made major over in Normandy. And I went overseas, I got promoted forward, which is not only up, but forward, which meant 'on the line.' I was the surgeon of an armored combat command.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about the treatment of Jewish and non-Jewish civilians in Europe at the time, before you went over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, there was-- there was no secret about what had been going on in Germany. I think we all knew about the Kristallnacht. And we knew that there's a place called Dachau. In fact, there was a movie made about-- that showed Dachau, a popular movie and I can't remember who all was in it, based on a novel by an English novelist. Nobody had any idea of the extent of the killing that was going on, the scale was what we-- we knew that there were-- that people-- that the treatment of Jews was, of course, brutal, that they were, to use a loose word, discriminated against, and that they were losing their property, that they were put into camps. And that's about as far as anybody knew. The-- the organized killing that went on was kind of a secret in the Western world, at least to me and my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you ship out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you ship out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We shipped over in September, '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ship were you on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was a liberating ship. Who knows what? They were little squat tubs that we sailed across. We sailed in a convoy, escorted by Canadian Corvettes, which are like in a cruiser. And we were shadowed by a German submarine wolfpack most of the way, and we could hear the depth bombs going off against the ships. We landed in England. And our division reassembled at the old British cavalry barracks near Salisbury, England, Salisbury Plain, classical military place. We spent a month in England picking up new tanks with higher velocity guns and retraining, and then we crossed the Channel, landed at Cherbourg. And Eisenhower couldn't quite decide which place-- which way to send our division. First, we heard we were going up north to Holland. But then they decided to send us in the big chase across France, chasing the Germans toward the German border. And we caught up with them toward the end of November, just as they turned and started their bloody fighting in the Siegfried Line and the Maginot Line positions.

speaker: interviewer
text: What major battles did your unit engage in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- the big ones we have battle stars for are called Ardennes-Alsace, Rhineland, Central Europe. They give you a campaign star for each campaign. We started out fighting our way through the old French Maginot Line forts from the wrong side, the ones that the French couldn't hold against the Germans. As the Germans retreated, they dug in their tanks behind these 20-foot concrete pill boxes. And we were attacking through heavy mud all along the German-Lorraine border. In our first battle, we went over the border and attacked the little place called Ottweiler, Germany, the first town in Germany that our division liberated. It was-- they have a big sign, you are entering Germany courtesy of the 12th Armored Division and so on. And then we stopped on the line, Christmas, and just heavy line slugging, through mud and pill boxes and burning villages, very heavy fighting. And then we were told to hold in place. We didn't know it, but the Belgian Bulge had broken out right on our left flank. So the division next to us, 3rd Army, went up to attack the Bulge. They were part of Patton's 3rd Army. We stretched our lines and held in place. And then when the Belgian Bulge slowed down, started to lose its impetus, Hitler and his generals switched to the Alsace-Lorraine, the second phase of the Bulge, to try and take it back. And we were alerted for movement. The 7th Army, which held that end of the front was a veteran group. They'd been in Italy. They'd fought their way up from Marseilles. They were well prepared. They didn't get caught on the wrong foot the way the boys did in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So part of their attempt to take Alsace back was a bridgehead across the Rhine, north of Strasbourg. Strasbourg, the capital of Alsace, is right on the Rhine. The Germans held a bridgehead below at the city of Colmar, a large piece of land. And they attacked several different directions. And one was, just-- what?-- 15 miles north of Strasbourg, across the Rhine, to link up with their other forces. So we went through the Vosges Mountains and attacked the Germans. And we were up-- our one division was up against something like three German divisions. It was heavy fighting, the coldest winter in 40 years, deep snow. And we stopped them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were down to our last 50 rounds of ammunition for the guns the night that they finally got halted. And we had lost one whole tank battalion, but we destroyed more German tanks than we lost. But it was-- and getting the wounded out was a nightmare sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any experiences--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --of getting the wounded out? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- what we-- the way we operated, battalion aid means you're on the line. I was the surgeon of an armored combat command. That's a third of an armored division, a battalion of tanks, a battalion of armored infantry, who ride in half-tracks with machine guns and mortars, a battalion of self-propelled artillery, and then supporting troops, like engineers and medics, 3,000 men. And each battalion had its own doctor, sometimes two for the-- and their own aid station. They'd been set up in ruined buildings or cellars pretty close to-- 300 or 400 yards from the front line. And we were getting shelled all the time, of course. And we'd have to wait until there was a break in shelling and then make a run for it with an ambulance, cram in the wounded, and then go. We had-- one of the terrifying things is when you have an aid station full of wounded men crying, groaning, and screaming, and then they start shelling it again. The roof starts coming in. It was very bloody fighting. We were up against SS troops, the 10th SS division. We learned that they were totally criminals. They trapped one of our aid men and killed him. I wrote about this in my book. There was an American wounded boy lying out in the snow crying for help, as the other aid men say, kind of like a little kid. And there had been a German machine gun covering the position, just random spurts. But one of the aid men, a brave little boy from New York-- excuse me-- said I can't stand to listen to that guy cry any more. I got to go get him. So he ran out waving a red cross at noon on bright snow, on a clear day. He knelt down and was bandaging him, and the SS man waited until he was all tied up, and then he cut him in two with a machine gun. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This came close to home, because one of our patrols crawled up and threw a hand grenade in. And the SS trooper tried to grab the hand grenade and it blew his hand off, so we got a hold of him. And I saw him at the rear aid station, where I happened to be driving through. And I didn't know what had happened. Here was this SS trooper, with a bloody stump. His right hand was gone. So I asked him the usual questions. I speak fluent German. And I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Heck with you, Herman. OK, we've taken care of you. And I shipped him out. But a little while later, the battalion surgeon came in looking to kill him, because they-- they knew who he was. Unfortunately, he got sent back to a nice big American hospital, where he got treated with love, tenderness, sympathy. He's probably back home in Germany telling them what a brave guy he was, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any other incidents while you were in these major battles that you'd like to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can remember the night the Germans did something stupid. That's always fun to think about. They somehow got the word that we had retreated all the way to the Vosges Mountains. We were down near the Rhine. And they came marching in, columns of fours, yelling commands right into our machine guns. And our tanks went out there and they were screaming, Doc, Doc, get up here, get up here! I was trying to get my ambulance toward the battle through a column of tanks. And I remember the fireworks flaring and the shells going off. Suddenly we were winning a battle, a very large one. But the hairiest part of that battle was the afternoon-- the day-- our last day on the line was the 19th of January in that battle. The Germans counterattacked with 50 tanks, Tiger tanks mostly, big ones, came right across the plain toward our command post. And we were all watching through field glasses. Our tanks were over on the other flank of the bridgehead, so there was nothing out there except a few machine gunners. And we-- our artillery observers called down artillery, and they knocked out some of the Germans. And our little headquarters detachment was out there with his one bazooka and a few machine guns and carbines to try and stop the 10th SS Panzer Division. And just in the nick of time, our tank battalion came roaring from the other flank, went out there and took them on and drove them off. The Rhine fog came in. There was a very tense thing that afternoon. That night we beat it off. The Germans veered off to the south, and one of-- another battalion of our tanks caught them in what we called defilade. They were down concealed and they knocked out a whole column of German tanks. So we stopped it for that day. But the German wounded, whom I talked to frequently, they told me everything. They were happy to be taken care of, so we got a lot of intelligence, that tomorrow comes the big attack. Tiger tanks, two battalions upon right here, right at this command. Please get me out of here, doctor. Get me out here to the rear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were all ready for them we thought. And then that was the time of the big ammunition shortage. The Pentagon miscalculated. After the victories in Normandy and France, they thought the war was going to be over, so they stopped shipping ammunition, if you can believe it. What year are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, '45. They thought-- they were over-optimistic. Suddenly our guns were short of ammunition. And we-- about 10 o'clock that night, we got the dismal news in our basement command post that there were only 50 more rounds of ammunition for all our guns and then we're out. The nearest resupply was Paris, 200 miles away. And we knew the German attack was coming the next morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was pretty tense along the line that night. Everyone was counting bullets. And then at-- we got a phone call and our intelligence officer, who was a Texan himself, started-- got almost hysterical he was so happy. The Texas National Guard, the 36th Infantry Division, which had been in reserve, 12,000 men, 15,000 men with tanks, guns, and stuff came marching up through the snow. We all ran outside to see these figures coming through the falling snow.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the nicest thing I've ever seen in my life. We went off the line-- out of 1,000 infantry man we went on the line with, we went off with 120 or 130 who were still walking when we left. That's in two weeks of fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your specific duties as a doctor? Were you a surgeon? Did you do surgery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we did front-line-- every doctor's called a surgeon in the Army. It doesn't mean you're a specialist or anything. We were front line. We would get the wounded, sometimes run out and get them ourselves, or go with the aid men, get them in some-- and get-- our job was to set up an aid station as close to the line as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: It came as a real shock in the Medical Field Service School in 1942 when they said, gentleman-- we all thought it would be in a hospital or something-- gentlemen, be sure to get the wounded in defilade. And we said, what's defilade? They said, that's out of the line of direct fire from machine guns. So we said, oh, yeah? OK. So you get them in shelter, like a cellar. In Europe, we'd find these ruined buildings, cellars, holes. There's always something to crawl into, cover it over, get a little stove going for heat for the wounded.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the aid men would bring them in on the half-tracks and litter Jeeps. We'd stop bleeding, splint bones, sometimes amputate limbs, and give blood plasma. They couldn't give blood that far forward in those days, but they had plasma, which was made up in a powdered bottle. And it-- you diluted it with a vacuum bottle. Then you could stick a soldier rifle on the ground by the bayonet, hang the plasma, and run it in by vein. We could do things like that. And very front line, very rough, immediate lifesaving intervention is what we did. And then we got them to the rear as fast as we could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have all the necessary supplies always that you needed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had-- well, we had to improvise. The old Army Medical Corps between wars was archaic. It was the-- the refuge of the incompetent. We had stuff that hadn't been used since the Spanish-American War in 1900. But we got good, modern stuff when we went overseas. Sulfa-- we had sulfa drugs-- didn't have penicillin yet-- and bandages and splints. And we improvised a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had anesthesia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- yes, we could use pentothal by vein, or chloroform, the old-fashioned anesthetic. Two or three quick whiffs of chloroform, you can set a bone quickly and let them come out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you deal with battle personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was scared all the time. But I-- you had to do what you had to do, as they say nowadays, so I did it. But it-- it's a terrifying experience, let me tell you. And it's worse and sicker-- as I described in my book, the first man I saw killed, when I suddenly realized what this is all about, was back in the line in December. I looked out the window of my aid station. A shell went off. There was a big explosion. Someone screamed, medics, medics.

speaker: interviewer
text: I grabbed my helmet and my aid kit and ran out across the field. And I yelled, where's the casualty? And the hands came up out of foxholes and pointed, over there, Doc. And then they all got back in their foxholes. I suddenly realized, when you're a medic, you go out and get the wounded.

speaker: interviewee
text: And an aid man and I got to this boy and his face was all blood. I was dabbing at it, looking for the wound. The aid man kept trying to babble at me. I couldn't understand what he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, Major, Major. I said, what? He said, he ain't got no back on his head. And we turned him over and the whole back of his head been sheared off, and his brains ran out in my lap. So we put him down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we knew that there would be another shell coming in in a minute. And sure enough, I dived-- I heard the next shell coming. I dived for the ground so fast, I dived out from under my helmet. And I ran back to the command post cellar diving under tanks and things. That's when you suddenly realize what war is, somebody's brains running out on the ground.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to stop this tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue with the next--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
